How can we help, or what would you like to share?
🧪 Beta Tester Program
Beta testers shape what CardiacLens becomes. In exchange for early input on new features, we ask for a simple weekly commitment.
What you're signing up for:
  • A weekly check-in prompt — answer honestly, even if it's “all good”
  • Two consecutive ignored check-ins = program complete, no re-enrollment
  • Your feedback goes directly to the developer (a cardiac patient like you)
  • No account, no personal data collected — same privacy as the app itself
⚠ This is a one-way commitment. Once you graduate (2 skips), you cannot re-enroll. Only join if you genuinely intend to respond each week.

Thank you for your time in the beta program — your feedback genuinely shaped CardiacLens. You can still reach us any time for support.

Help, About & Privacy
‹
›
🔍

Welcome to CardiacLens

Free Cardiac Health Tracking
Built by a cardiac patient, for cardiac patients. No account, no subscription, no ads — ever. Your data never leaves your device.
cardiaclens.com
Scan to open CardiacLens on any device
What CardiacLens does

CardiacLens logs your blood pressure, heart rate, weight, fluid intake, symptoms, medications, meals, and activities — then analyzes the patterns over time so you and your cardiologist have real evidence to work from, not just a single office reading.

Four Advanced Analytics detectors continuously watch for clinically meaningful changes: sustained heart rate shifts, decompensation warning signs, pulse pressure trends, and symptom convergence patterns. Sentinel goes further — it watches for the personal pre-event patterns that came before your own prior clinical events, not population averages. When any tool finds something significant, it tells you what was found and why it matters — in plain language.

⚠️ Important — What CardiacLens is not

CardiacLens is a pattern awareness tool, not a diagnostic device. It does not diagnose, prescribe, or replace your care team. All findings are described as directional — they point toward patterns worth discussing with your cardiologist, never toward a conclusion. The phrase you will see throughout the app: "Math is directional, not definitive."

What's in this app
📊 HR Rate Detector
Detects sustained heart rate shifts and rapid changes over time
🚨 Decompensation Warning
Monitors 5 clinical signs of fluid overload building before a crisis
🩺 Pulse Pressure Detector
Tracks the gap between systolic and diastolic BP as a cardiac output indicator
🔍 Symptom Convergence
Finds clusters, escalations, and clinical patterns across your symptoms
🛡️ Sentinel
Watches for the patterns that came before your prior clinical events — personal, not population-based
📌 Pinned Events
Permanently preserves significant findings for your cardiologist appointment
🌅 Morning Check-In
45-second daily pattern log — builds your fluid response profile and overnight symptom record
📅 60-Day Timeline
Color-coded calendar of cardiac events — designed for doctor appointments
💬 Ask CardiacLens
AI-powered plain-English questions about your logged data, patterns, and what to tell your doctor
🥦 Food Check
Nutritional awareness for cardiac and kidney patients — evaluates foods against your conditions and medications
🍲 Meal Builder
Build complete meals item by item, see running nutritional totals, analyze the combined load, and save safe meals to your personal library
🍽️ Manage Meals
View, edit, re-evaluate, flag, and delete your saved meals library — accessible from the main page
How to navigate this guide
  • Getting Started — Your name, your conditions, and your doctor's BP targets. Then start logging — the app guides you from there.
  • How to Use — Daily workflow, doctor appointment prep, and the five detector tools
  • 📌 Pinned Events — Your most powerful clinical tool for doctor appointments
  • 🌅 Morning Check-In — How fluid and lifestyle pattern tracking works
  • 🌡️ Heat Advisory — What to do on hot or humid days as a cardiac patient
  • 🛡️ Sentinel — Personal pattern precursor — watches for what came before your prior events
  • 💬 Ask — Ask plain-English questions about your logged data, detector findings, or any CardiacLens feature — AI-powered, privacy-preserving. Includes 🥦 Food Check and 🍲 Meal Builder for nutritional awareness specific to your conditions and medications
  • 🍽️ Manage Meals — View, edit, re-evaluate, flag Do Not Use, and delete your saved meals library
  • FAQ — Common questions answered plainly
  • 📖 Glossary — Definitions for every clinical term and app phrase used in CardiacLens
  • Data Mgmt — How to back up, export, and protect your data
  • Privacy — How your data is stored, who can see it, and the medical disclaimer
  • 🔒 Security — Set up Secure Access to lock the app with a private key letter
  • Known Issues — Bug fix history. If something seems wrong, check here first.
  • ☕ Support — CardiacLens is free. If it helps you, a small contribution means a lot.

Welcome to CardiacLens

CardiacLens adapts to you — your conditions, your doctor's targets, your care plan. It needs three things from you first. After that, just start logging. The app will guide you to everything else as your data builds.

Three steps — takes about 2 minutes
1
Open Settings and enter your name
Your name appears on every report your doctor sees. Settings → Patient Profile.
2
Select your cardiac conditions
CardiacLens uses these to tailor alerts, reports, and symptom suggestions to your diagnosis. Settings → Patient Profile.
3
Enter your doctor's BP targets if you have them
If you don't know them yet, leave the defaults — you can update them anytime. Settings → App Settings.
📋 What to log

Your doctor asked you to track your blood pressure — start there. Log it every time you take a reading using the Log Blood Pressure button on the main screen.

If your doctor also mentioned fluid intake, weight, or symptoms — turn those on in Settings → Tracking Features. Each one adds a button to the main screen.

Everything else can wait. The more you log, the more CardiacLens learns about your patterns — and it will let you know when a new feature is ready to be useful to you.

💡 How CardiacLens guides you

As your data builds, Sentinel — the status banner on your main screen — will periodically surface one suggestion at a time. Not a list of everything the app can do. Just the one thing that's most useful to you right now, based on what you've already logged. Tap the banner anytime to see it.

💡 Keep your reminders firing reliably

CardiacLens reminders run on a JavaScript timer. The screen must stay on and lit — a black screen suspends the timer. Low Power Mode must be OFF — it throttles timers even in the foreground.

Airplane Mode is fine — all logging, reminders, and monitoring work offline. Only Ask requires internet.

This is an iOS platform restriction (Safari suspends background JavaScript for installed web apps), not a CardiacLens limitation — see the FAQ for how this compares to Android.

🔒 Protect your health data with Secure Access

CardiacLens can lock the entire app behind a private key letter. When enabled, the app opens to what looks like a numeric PIN screen — anyone who doesn't know your key letter will tap numbers and never get in.

Set it up in Settings → 🔒 Secure Access. Takes less than a minute. See the FAQ and Privacy tabs for full details.

Already familiar with CardiacLens?

Use the full setup guide below to configure everything at once — medications, daily schedule, notifications, and advanced features.

How to Use

Daily Workflow — 5 minutes or less
  1. Morning Check-In — Tap the yellow banner at the top each morning. 45 seconds. Records how you feel and yesterday's context for pattern tracking.
  2. Log BP — Use the Log Blood Pressure button after taking your reading. Tag it to a medication or activity if relevant.
  3. Log Fluid — Every time you drink something, tap Log Fluid or use the event buttons. The progress bar updates in real time.
  4. Log Meds — Mark each scheduled medication as taken when you take it. CardiacLens tracks adherence automatically.
  5. Events fire as reminders — Set up timed events in Settings (Morning Meds, Lunch, Evening Walk, etc.) and the app will prompt you at each one.
Before a Doctor Appointment
  1. Tap the 🔍 Deep Dive tile in Today's Status — set the date range to cover since your last visit. Read the plain English summary at the top, then check the Convergence section for days where multiple concerns appeared together. These are the most important things to discuss.
  2. Tap Ask CardiacLens About This Period at the bottom of Deep Dive — the AI has full context for your selected date range and can help you prepare specific questions for your doctor.
  3. Open Advanced Analytics — tap 🩺 Doctor's Report for a formatted clinical summary of BP trends, medication adherence, symptoms, fluid behavior, and morning patterns. It generates immediately for the selected date range (or the last 30 days if none is selected) — no need to run Generate Analysis first.
  4. Review your 📌 Pinned Events — bring any pinned findings to the appointment. The Pinned Events viewer shows the full context of each finding.
🛡️ Sentinel — Personal Pattern Precursor

Sentinel is separate from the four detectors and works differently. Instead of watching for population-based thresholds, it watches for your personal pre-event patterns. Every time you pin a clinical event, Sentinel extracts what your data looked like in the 72 hours before it — your Footprint. When your live data resembles a prior Footprint, Sentinel tells you which event it matches and how. Tap 🛡️ Sentinel on the main screen to open it. See the 🛡️ Sentinel tab in this guide for the full explanation.

Keeping Your Data Safe
  1. Set up a weekly backup event in Settings — it will remind you every week and let you export with one tap from the notification.
  2. Use Device Sync & Backup → Export ALL Data before updating the app or switching devices.
  3. If you use CardiacLens as a Home Screen icon on iPhone — always export before deleting the icon, as removing it erases all local data.
Tips
  • Turn features on/off in Settings based on what your doctor has asked you to track.
  • Set your BP target ranges in Settings so the app can color-code readings correctly.
  • The Fluid Budget Dashboard lets you allocate your daily fluid goal across events so you pace intake throughout the day.
  • Log symptoms with a severity rating — the Doctor's Report uses this to flag high-severity episodes for your cardiologist.
  • Activity Timer: Use the timer in Log Activity to time workouts in real time. Tap ⏸ Pause to freeze the clock if interrupted — a PAUSED label confirms it's stopped. Tap ▶ Resume to continue, then ⏹ Stop & Use to save the full elapsed time.
  • HR Rate Detector — Quick Stats: Open HR Rate Detector and use the Today / 7 Days / 30 Days / All Time buttons to analyse your heart rate trends across any time window. The chart scrolls horizontally for multi-day views and shows date labels so you can pinpoint when patterns occurred.
  • What are cards? Cards are the main sections on your home screen — Blood Pressure, Weight Tracking, Symptom Tracking, Activity, Meals, Medicines Taken, Fluid Intake, HR Rate Detector, and Notes. Each card shows today's data by default. Tap a tile in Today's Status to open its card. Tap ▲ Collapse to close it again.
  • Viewing history for any card: Each card has a 📋 View History button in its header. Tap it to see up to 90 days of entries with filters, search, and sort options. Tap ← Back to Today to return. Only one card shows history at a time.
  • Correcting past weight entries: Tap 📋 View History on the Weight card header. A Jump to date picker lets you navigate directly to any date. Entries that deviate significantly from your 7-day rolling average are automatically flagged — yellow ⚠️ for 10–19 lbs off, red 🚨 for 20+ lbs — so data entry errors are easy to spot without scrolling through every record.
📊 Advanced Analytics — Four Detectors + Sentinel
Four detectors analyze your historical data for patterns easy to miss day to day. Sentinel watches for what came before your prior events.
📊 HR Rate Detector

Analyzes your full HR history to find level shifts — periods where your resting heart rate durably changed to a new baseline — and rapid changes — sudden single-session jumps. Requires 6+ days of HR readings. Use the Today / 7 Days / 30 Days / All Time buttons to narrow the window. A "🔍 Investigate" button on any finding opens Anchor Date Investigation for that exact date.

🚨 Decompensation Warning

Monitors five clinical signals simultaneously: weight gain, cardiac output (HR + pulse pressure), symptom frequency, fluid intake patterns, and notes keyword scan. Each signal is scored individually. The composite result ranges from ✅ All Clear → 📋 Early Indicator (1 signal) → ⚠️ Monitor Closely (2+ signals) → 🚨 Contact Cardiologist (multiple red signals). One signal alone is not cause for alarm — the value is in seeing multiple signals converge.

🩺 Pulse Pressure Detector

Pulse pressure is systolic minus diastolic BP. Normal range is 40–60 mmHg. A narrowing trend can indicate the heart is working harder to maintain output; a widening trend may indicate arterial stiffness. Many cardiac patients have a chronically reduced PP that is their personal baseline — the detector recognizes this and labels it "Chronically Narrow — Your Stable Baseline" rather than an acute alert. What matters is change from your baseline, not comparison to a textbook number.

🔍 Symptom Convergence

Analyzes 30 days of symptom logs across three layers: (1) Intraday clusters — 3+ symptoms within a 4-hour window on the same day; (2) Multi-day escalation — the same symptom worsening in severity across multiple days; (3) Clinical triads — recognized symptom combinations with known cardiac significance (e.g., shortness of breath + fatigue + edema). The 60-day Timeline tab shows where clusters occurred visually.

📌 Pinned Events — Your Cardiologist Preparation Tool

When any detector finds a significant event (yellow or red), it suggests pinning it. Pinned events are saved permanently — they never roll off with the 90-day data window. Each pin captures the detector's full finding plus all raw log data for that date.

Pinned events appear in three places: the 📌 Pinned Events button in Analytics, the 60-day Timeline as amber markers, and your Doctor's Report as a dedicated section.

After your cardiologist appointment: use "Archive & Clear" to permanently save the current pins and start fresh for the next period. An appointment nudge reminds you automatically.

⚠️ About accuracy — read this once

All four detectors use statistical pattern detection on self-reported data. The math is directional, not definitive. A finding means: "this pattern is present in your data and worth discussing." It does not mean: "something is wrong." Findings can reflect real clinical changes, data entry errors, device variability, or normal day-to-day fluctuation. Your cardiologist provides the clinical interpretation — CardiacLens provides the data and the pattern recognition to make that conversation more productive.

🔒 Medication Safety System

CardiacLens includes built-in safety checks that can block a medication dose before it is logged. These checks run automatically every time you tap Save in Log Medicine.

🚫 BP Reading Required

If a medicine has a condition check set, a blood pressure reading within the last 4 hours is required before the dose can be logged. If no recent reading exists, a block screen appears with a Log BP Now button. After logging BP, return to Log Medicine — your previous selection is automatically restored.

🚫 Condition Not Met

If your current BP or heart rate reading fails a medicine's condition threshold, the dose is blocked. The block screen shows the medicine name, what the condition requires, your actual current reading, and all three vitals against your target ranges. Use the Update Condition button if the threshold needs adjusting, or tap Back to return to Log Medicine.

🚫 Too Soon — Minimum Interval

Each medicine has a minimum time between doses based on its schedule — twice daily means 6 hours minimum, once daily means 12 hours, every 2 days means 48 hours. If your last logged dose was too recent, the block screen shows exactly when you last took it and how long you must wait. Never double dose — if you missed a dose and the next one is close, skip and resume your normal schedule.

⚠️ Condition Warning (Caution Zone)

When your reading falls between your warn threshold and your block threshold, a caution screen appears. You can still log the dose — but you must acknowledge the risk first. This is designed for situations where the reading is low but not critically so, and your judgment matters. Warn thresholds are set in the Medication Intelligence panel, not in Manage Medicines.

⚠️ Pulse Pressure Warning

If your most recent pulse pressure (systolic minus diastolic) is below your approved PP warn threshold, a separate caution screen appears before logging certain medications. This is especially relevant for beta blockers and ARBs, which can further narrow PP. Your PP warn threshold and recovery target are set in the Medication Intelligence panel.

⚠️ Elevated Heart Rate Warning

If your average HR over the last 3 readings is above your target ceiling (default 80 bpm, or your MIP-approved HR warn threshold), a caution screen appears. A single high reading is not sufficient to trigger this — three consecutive elevated readings are required, which is the clinically appropriate threshold for sustained elevation.

⚙️ Setting Up a Condition Check

Open Manage Medicines and tap Edit on any medicine. Scroll to the Condition Check section at the bottom. Select the vital to check (Diastolic BP, Systolic BP, or Heart Rate), enter a threshold number, and choose whether to block when the reading is below or above that threshold. The condition note is generated automatically from your settings — no manual text entry needed. For dynamic thresholds derived from your own data, use the Medication Intelligence panel instead.

📅 Every X Days Schedule

For medications taken every other day or on a similar pattern, select Every X days (custom) in the frequency dropdown and enter the number of days. Every other day = 2. The minimum interval block uses this number directly — a medicine set to every 2 days requires 48 hours between logged doses.

💊 Changing a Medicine Name

When you edit a medicine and change its name, CardiacLens asks what changed. Choose Correcting a typo to update all history to the new name. Choose New dose or formulation to keep history under the old name, mark the old medicine as Discontinued today, and start tracking the new name from today. This preserves your before/after history for clinical analysis.

⚠️ Data Integrity Banner

If a red banner appears at the top of the app saying safety checks are disabled, a data integrity problem was detected. This may be caused by a storage error or data edited outside the app.

To restore safety checks: delete any BP readings or medications logged today and re-enter them through the app. Safety checks re-enable automatically on the next save. If the banner keeps appearing, export your backup and contact support at support@cardiaclens.com.

🧠 Medication Intelligence Panel

🧠
Thresholds from your data — not population averages
The Medication Intelligence Panel computes personalized block and warn thresholds for each medication using your own BP, HR, and pulse pressure history — then lets you review and approve them before they activate.
Why Hardcoded Thresholds Are Insufficient

A threshold that was correct in March may be wrong in August. Your clinical picture shifts — pacemaker adjustments, medication holds, seasonal changes, acute illness. A fixed number baked into the app has no way to know when your baseline has moved. The Medication Intelligence Panel solves this by computing thresholds from your actual recent data and surfacing them for your review.

📊 Three Time Windows

The panel displays your data across three windows simultaneously:

  • 7-day — what is happening right now. Acute shifts, medication holds, crisis periods. This is the primary driver for threshold suggestions.
  • 30-day — your recent stable compensated baseline. Shows where you normally run.
  • 90-day — the longer arc. Seasonal patterns, post-procedure recovery, medication titration effects over time. Without this context, a 7-day anomaly looks permanent when it may be temporary.
🔢 How Thresholds Are Calculated

The panel uses a clinically validated method based on your personal mean and standard deviation:

  • Block threshold = mean − 1 SD — the lower control limit. Below this, your reading has moved one full standard deviation below your personal average. This is the clinically accepted threshold for triggering an alert.
  • Warn threshold = mean − 0.5 SD — the caution zone. Your reading is trending low but has not yet crossed the block line. Surfaces a warning, not a hard stop.

These numbers are derived from research on home blood pressure monitoring and individual BP variability published in peer-reviewed hypertension literature. A coefficient of variation of approximately 9.9% for systolic BP in home monitoring settings supports using ±1 SD as a meaningful clinical boundary.

✅ You Approve Everything — Then It Syncs Automatically

The panel suggests — you decide. Nothing changes in the medication safety system until you tap Approve & Activate. You can also override any suggested threshold with your own number. Your personal clinical judgment — informed by your doctor's guidance — always takes precedence over the algorithm.

When you approve a threshold, CardiacLens automatically syncs it into the medicine's condition rules. A block rule is created (or updated) at the block threshold, and a warn rule is created at the warn threshold — so the condition rule engine and the Intelligence Panel are always in agreement. If you have already set a manual rule for the same metric, its threshold is updated in-place while preserving your chosen outcome (block or warn).

Approved PP thresholds sync a warn rule across all active medicines automatically. You can review any synced rule in Manage Medicines and adjust it at any time — manual edits are never overwritten by the MIP.

📐 Pulse Pressure Tracking

Pulse pressure (systolic minus diastolic) is tracked separately in the panel. PP is a surrogate for stroke volume — narrow PP suggests the heart is pumping less effectively per beat. The panel lets you set a PP warn threshold and a recovery target, which are used to surface a caution screen before logging medications known to affect PP.

For cardiac patients, a PP consistently below 25 mmHg is associated with reduced cardiac output. The panel tracks your personal PP baseline so your threshold is grounded in your own data, not population norms.

🔒 Condition Rules — Multi-Rule Engine

Each medicine can now have up to 4 condition rules. Each rule checks one vital sign (Systolic BP, Diastolic BP, Heart Rate, or Pulse Pressure) against a threshold and either blocks or warns when the reading crosses it.

  • OR logic — any single rule that fires triggers its outcome. Use this for independent safety conditions, such as: block if diastolic below 65 OR block if systolic below 95.
  • AND logic — all rules must be true simultaneously. Use this for compound patterns that only matter when they occur together.
  • Block — prevents logging until the condition passes. For example, Losartan: block if systolic below 100 (hospital discharge instruction).
  • Warn — shows a caution screen but allows logging with acknowledgement. For example, HR below 55 on a beta blocker — worth noticing, not necessarily a reason to skip the dose.

Rules are set in Manage Medicines — tap any medicine and scroll to the Condition Rules section. The rule builder shows a plain-English preview before saving. Legacy single-condition medicines migrate automatically. Rules created by the Intelligence Panel are labelled MIP-approved with the approval date so you can always tell them apart from rules you wrote manually.

💊 Drug Class Grouping

You can tag each medicine with its drug class (Beta Blocker, ARB, SGLT2 Inhibitor, etc.). CardiacLens auto-suggests the class from the medicine name — you confirm or change it. The class is used in the Intelligence Panel to group related medicines and offer to copy condition rules when two medicines share the same class.

Example: if you set up Metoprolol 12.5mg with block rules and later add Metoprolol 25mg (also tagged Beta Blocker), the panel offers one tap to copy the same rules over — then you adjust the thresholds for the new dose.

Important: The Medication Intelligence Panel is a decision-support tool — not a prescription system. All threshold suggestions are directional and based on self-reported data. Discuss any threshold changes with your cardiologist before activating them. CardiacLens does not provide medical advice and all findings are patterns worth discussing with your care team.
🔍 Finding a medicine quickly (v9.10.209)

If you're managing several medications, use the search box at the top of the panel to jump straight to the one you need — type a medicine name (e.g. "Eliquis") or a drug class (e.g. "Beta Blocker") and the list filters as you type. Drug-class groups stay together so you still see related medicines side by side.

On longer lists, a small ⬆️ button appears in the bottom-right corner once you scroll down — tap it to jump back to the top of the panel without scrolling.

🔍 Deep Dive — Your Complete Health Picture

🔍
One tap. Every card. Any time period.
Deep Dive brings all your health data together in one view so you can see what was happening across your whole health picture — not just one number at a time.
📋 First — what are cards?

When you open CardiacLens, the main screen shows sections that each track one part of your health. Each section is called a card. There are nine cards:

📊
Blood Pressure — your BP and heart rate readings
⚖️
Weight Tracking — your daily weight with trend detection
💊
Symptom Tracking — symptoms you've logged with severity ratings
🏃
Activity / Exercise — workouts and physical activity
🍽️
Meals — what you ate and when
💊
Medicines Taken — your medication log with timing
💧
Fluid Intake — water and fluids logged throughout the day
⚡
HR Rate Detector — heart rate pattern analysis
📝
Notes — your personal observations and daily notes

Each card shows today's data by default. Tap a tile in Today's Status to open a card. Each card has a 📋 View History button to see up to 90 days of that card's data with search and filter options.

Why Deep Dive is different from viewing cards individually

Each card only shows its own data. The BP card shows your blood pressure. The Symptoms card shows your symptoms. But your health doesn't work in separate columns — everything is connected.

Example: A day that looks fine card by card
📊 Blood Pressure card — one reading slightly high. Seems minor.
💧 Fluid Intake card — below your daily goal. Could be a coincidence.
💊 Symptoms card — one unexpected symptom logged. Easy to miss.

Deep Dive Convergence: 🚨 All three happened on the same day.
That pattern is worth bringing to your cardiologist — even if each item alone seemed minor.
How to open Deep Dive
🔍
Tap the 🔍 Deep Dive tile in Today's Status — it's always visible in the tile grid regardless of whether you've logged anything today. The tile is dark navy so it stands out.
Once open, choose a time period using the buttons at the top: Today, Last 7 Days, Last 30 Days, Last 90 Days, or set a Custom date range by tapping Custom and entering From and To dates.
What you'll see in Deep Dive
🤖 CardiacLens Summary
Appears at the very top. 2–3 plain English sentences summarizing the most important things from your selected period. Generated by AI from your actual data. Not medical advice — just a clear, human-readable summary of what your numbers show.
💡 Suggested Analyses
CardiacLens automatically suggests specific date windows worth investigating — based on your pinned events, medicine changes, symptom clusters, and periods of high concern. Tap Analyse → on any suggestion to load that period instantly.
📊 Data sections for each card
Each card's data is summarized with averages, counts, and key facts for the period you selected. Blood pressure averages. Weight change. Symptom frequency. Activity days. Fluid vs target. Medicine adherence per drug.
🔗 Convergence — the most important section
Shows specific days where multiple concerns appeared at the same time.

⚠️ Amber — 2 concerns on the same day (e.g. BP high + no fluid logged)
🚨 Red — 3 or more concerns on the same day

Each flagged day lists exactly what was off. These are the dates most worth discussing with your doctor.
How real users use Deep Dive
📅 Before a doctor appointment
Set the date range to cover since your last visit. Read the plain English summary. Check the Convergence section — those are your talking points. Tap Ask CardiacLens About This Period to prepare specific questions. Show the convergence findings on your phone during the appointment.
💊 After a medication change
Use the Suggested Analyses panel — CardiacLens will automatically suggest a before/after comparison centered on your medicine start date. Tap Analyse → to see if your BP, symptoms, or HR changed after starting the new medication.
📌 After a pacemaker adjustment or procedure
If you've pinned that event, it will appear in Suggested Analyses automatically. The suggested window puts the procedure date in the middle so you can compare the 14 days before and after across all your health data at once.
🤔 "I've been feeling off lately"
Open Deep Dive and tap Last 30 Days. Read the summary. Look at the Convergence section — it will show you which specific days had the most things going wrong simultaneously. Then ask CardiacLens: "What patterns stand out in the last 30 days that I should mention to my doctor?"
💊 Getting medicine adherence right

Deep Dive shows how consistently you logged each medicine. For the numbers to be accurate, each medicine needs to be set up correctly in your Medicine List.

Active — you take this medicine on a regular schedule
Conditional — you only take it when certain conditions are met (e.g. "only when systolic > 100"). Deep Dive shows your condition note instead of a percentage.
Discontinued — you have stopped taking this. Deep Dive marks it as discontinued and excludes it from adherence calculations.
Same drug as — if you take the same medication at different doses (e.g. two different Eliquis doses), link them. Deep Dive counts either one as a dose taken.
⚠️ Deep Dive is only as good as what you log

Deep Dive can only work with data you've logged. A day where you took your medicine but forgot to log it shows as a missed dose. A symptom you didn't log isn't counted.

You don't need to be perfect. Logging BP once a day and medicines daily gives CardiacLens enough to find meaningful patterns. Consistency over weeks matters more than completeness on any single day.

Quick setup — get Deep Dive working in 5 minutes
1. Set your BP target range
Used to flag readings outside your target
2. Set your daily fluid goal
Used to flag days where fluid was below target
3. Review your Medicine List
Set status and start date for each medicine
4. Open Deep Dive
Tap 🔍 Deep Dive in Today's Status — try Last 30 Days first

📌 Pinned Events — Your Most Powerful Clinical Tool

What a Pinned Event Is

A Pinned Event is a flagged finding from one of CardiacLens's four detectors — captured permanently, along with the full raw data behind it, so it never disappears when your 90-day rolling window advances. It is the difference between "I thought I noticed something" and "here is the exact date, the exact reading, and every data point around it."

Why Convergence Is the Key

Any single detector firing on any single day is background noise. What CardiacLens watches for is convergence — two or more independent detectors flagging the same time window from different directions. When your HR level-shifts and your pulse pressure narrows and your symptom frequency rises in the same week, that combination is clinically meaningful in a way that no individual reading can be.

A single yellow flag: worth watching. Three yellow flags on the same week: worth showing your cardiologist.

🩺 How to Use It in the Doctor's Office

Open Pinned Events before your appointment. Each detector-backed pin card now shows a “What to Say at Your Appointment” box with a pre-written sentence generated directly from your detector data. You do not have to figure out how to explain it — just read it aloud exactly as written.

📋 What the box looks like:
Each pin card with unresolved detector findings shows an orange box labeled “What to Say at Your Appointment.” The sentence inside is auto-generated from your data — the exact detector type, values, and timeframe — and is not editable. Read it aloud to your cardiologist exactly as written.
Box status rules:
🟠 Orange box (Unresolved) — read aloud at your appointment
🟡 Muted box (Deferred) — on record, monitor for future visits
✅ No box (Addressed) — your cardiologist has reviewed this finding

After reading, ask: “Does this line up with anything you’re seeing in my chart? Is it worth investigating further, or is it within normal variation for my condition?”

📝 Why the Language Is Read-Only

The sentences in the “What to Say” box are generated entirely from detector data — not from your written notes or your memory. You cannot edit them, and that is by design.

This means the app stands behind every word it generates. If you choose to say something different at your appointment, that is your right — but the documented, data-backed language is always there as your record. A cardiologist reading a timestamped, algorithm-generated sentence weighted by your personal baseline carries more clinical credibility than a patient’s self-description.

🔍 From a Real Patient — Shared With Permission

A patient using CardiacLens chose to share their experience and gave permission for it to be discussed here. They have a pacemaker and had been logging their heart rate daily for several weeks. The HR Rate Detector identified a durable level shift — a point where the resting heart rate had measurably settled into a new, stable baseline distinctly different from the weeks before it. The change wasn't a single bad reading. It was a sustained pattern across multiple days that the algorithm confirmed as statistically significant.

The pacemaker's remote monitoring system reviewed the same time period during its scheduled download. It did not flag the shift. The clinical team saw nothing requiring follow-up.

The patient brought the Pinned Event to their next cardiology appointment and read it aloud. The cardiologist reviewed the supporting data, confirmed the shift was real, and ordered follow-up testing. The window between device downloads — where most of daily life happens — is exactly the gap that self-logged, multi-stream data is built to fill.

📅 When to Archive vs. When to Keep
  • Archive after your appointment — once your cardiologist has reviewed the pins and either acted on them or noted them, use "Archive & Clear" to permanently save that period and start fresh for the next visit cycle.
  • Keep active during a workup — if tests are pending or a diagnosis is not yet finalized, leave pins in place. They are part of the active clinical record and should remain readable until you have an answer.
  • Mark addressed, don't delete — if you've discussed a pin and your doctor said it was benign, mark it Addressed rather than deleting it. The historical context is still valuable if a similar pattern returns later.
✏️ Creating a Manual Pin

You can now create a pin directly — without needing a detector finding. Tap the ✏️ Add button in the Pinned Events modal header to open the manual pin form.

Manual pins are ideal for: questions you want to raise at your next appointment, observations that don't come from a detector (a symptom pattern you noticed, a concern after a medication change), or anything clinically important you want to preserve and surface at your next visit.

  • Title (required) — what you want to remember or discuss
  • Date (required) — the date this observation applies to
  • Summary / Notes — as much detail as you want to preserve
  • Doctor (optional) — which doctor this is intended for

Manual pins appear with a ✏️ Manual Entry label and support all pin features — communication log, question list, status tracking, and inclusion in the Doctor's Report.

What Pinned Events Are NOT: They are not a diagnosis. They are not an alarm. A pinned event does not mean something is wrong — it means a pattern was detected that your cardiologist should know about. The math is directional, not definitive. Your cardiologist provides the clinical interpretation; CardiacLens provides the documented evidence to make that conversation productive.

🫁 Breathing Logger

What this feature is — and where it came from

On the morning this feature was designed, the builder logged a wake HR of 81 bpm. After 5 minutes of extended-exhale breathing, HR was 67 bpm — a drop of 14 bpm with no medication, no equipment, and no cost. That documented response, in a cardiac patient with a pacemaker, is the entire reason this feature exists.

The Breathing Logger is a pattern awareness tool and data logger. It guides you through a timed breathing session, logs your HR before and after, tracks your personal response over time, and includes that data in your Doctor's Report. It is not a treatment, not a prescription, and not a substitute for your care team. It is a way to capture something real that is otherwise invisible.

The clinical basis — what research shows

How it works physiologically: When you exhale slowly and fully, the vagus nerve — a long nerve running from the brainstem to the heart — sends a parasympathetic signal that tells the sinoatrial node (your heart's natural pacemaker) to slow down. This is called the vagal brake. The longer the exhale relative to the inhale, the stronger this braking effect. This is not a theory — it is the same mechanism used in formal cardiac rehabilitation programs.

Heart rate variability (HRV): HRV is the variation in time between heartbeats. Higher HRV generally means your autonomic nervous system is flexible and responsive — a sign of cardiac resilience. Low HRV is an independent predictor of poor outcomes in heart failure patients. Slow, paced breathing — especially at around 6 breaths per minute (the 5-5 resonance frequency) — has been shown in multiple studies to acutely increase HRV. Some cardiac rehabilitation programs use this technique explicitly.

Sympathetic overdrive in CHF: In heart failure, the sympathetic nervous system is chronically overactivated — the body tries to compensate for reduced cardiac output by raising HR and constricting blood vessels. This compensation is initially helpful but becomes harmful over time. Extended-exhale breathing directly counters sympathetic overdrive by activating the opposing parasympathetic system. This is why the effect can be surprisingly large in cardiac patients.

CardiacLens does not claim this technique treats your condition. The research cited here is the reason the feature was built as a logger — not a prescriber. Your cardiologist interprets what your personal response data means for your health.

The three techniques — what each one does

The rule behind all three: The exhale must be at least 1.5× to 2× the inhale to activate the vagal brake. Equal breathing (1:1 ratio) produces little parasympathetic effect. The longer and slower the exhale, the stronger the signal to the sinoatrial node.

4-6 Extended Exhale — Start here
Timing: Inhale for 4 seconds, exhale for 6 seconds. One cycle = 10 seconds. At 5 minutes, you complete approximately 30 cycles.
Ratio: 1:1.5 — the minimum effective ratio.
Who it's for: Everyone using this feature for the first time. Easy to learn, comfortable to sustain, and clinically effective. The 4-second inhale is short enough that most people don't feel air-hunger during the exhale.
What to expect: Most people feel a sense of calm settling in around cycle 5–8. HR response typically begins within 2–3 minutes.
5-5 Resonance Breathing — The heart failure technique
Timing: Inhale for 5 seconds, exhale for 5 seconds. One cycle = 10 seconds, same as 4-6.
Ratio: 1:1 — but the significance here is not the ratio, it's the frequency. At exactly 6 breaths per minute, breathing synchronizes with the natural oscillation of the cardiovascular system (the Mayer wave) in a phenomenon called cardiovascular resonance. This produces larger swings in HR with each breath and is associated with the greatest acute HRV increase of the three techniques.
Who it's for: People who have already tried 4-6 and want to explore the technique most studied in heart failure patients. Also useful if you find the unequal ratio of 4-6 hard to maintain naturally.
What to expect: You may notice a stronger sense of your heart rate rising and falling with each breath — this is the resonance effect and is normal. Some people find this more calming than 4-6; others find it less so. Try both and let your data decide.
4-7-8 Deep Calm — Advanced technique
Timing: Inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 7 seconds, exhale for 8 seconds. One cycle = 19 seconds. At 5 minutes you complete approximately 15–16 cycles.
Ratio: 1:2 exhale-to-inhale — the strongest vagal activation of the three.
Who it's for: People who have practiced 4-6 regularly and want a more intense session. The 7-second hold requires practice — it can feel uncomfortable at first, especially if you have any lung disease.
Who should be cautious: Anyone with COPD, moderate or severe lung disease, recent chest surgery, or who experiences anxiety from breath-holding. If the hold causes distress, return to 4-6.
What to expect: The hold phase activates a secondary vagal mechanism through intrathoracic pressure changes. HR response is often larger than 4-6, but so is the effort required. Not recommended as a first technique.
Duration settings — what each one means
  • 3 minutes (~18 cycles of 4-6): A minimal effective dose. Enough to produce a measurable vagal response in most people. Good for days when time is short or you're logging a quick check on how your body is responding.
  • 5 minutes (~30 cycles of 4-6): The recommended starting duration. Most of the clinical research on paced breathing uses 5 minutes as the standard measurement window. Your pre-to-post HR comparison at 5 minutes is comparable to data in published studies.
  • 10 minutes (~60 cycles of 4-6): Extended session. Produces the largest and most sustained HRV effect. Some cardiac rehab programs use 10–20 minutes daily. Best used once you're comfortable with the technique and have confirmed you tolerate it well.
  • Custom: Enter any duration from 1–120 minutes. Useful if your cardiologist suggests a specific duration, or if you want to match a protocol you've read about. Note: the 30-second minimum still applies regardless of what you enter.

On the timer: The active session shows a countdown ("4:13 remaining") in large teal text so you always know where you are. The number turns amber in the final 60 seconds. A progress bar tracks overall completion. The cycle counter shows how many complete breath cycles you've done.

Who should consider using this

This technique is most likely to show a measurable response in people with:

  • Elevated resting HR (above ~75 bpm): The vagal brake only works if there is sympathetic tone to counteract. If your HR is already low, the response will be smaller — this is not a failure.
  • Heart failure (CHF): Sympathetic overdrive is a core feature of CHF. This is the population with the most published research on paced breathing and HRV.
  • Hypertension: Slow paced breathing has shown modest blood pressure-lowering effects in short-term studies, likely through the same vagal mechanism.
  • Stress-related HR elevations: If you notice your HR climbs with anxiety, stress, or poor sleep, a breathing session can provide a measurable, logged counterweight.
  • Anyone wanting to build useful data for their cardiologist: Even if your response is small, consistent logging creates a pattern — and patterns are what CardiacLens is built to find.

Always confirm with your cardiologist before starting any new self-management practice, particularly if your condition has changed recently.

Who should not use this without cardiologist guidance
  • Recent hospitalization (within 4 weeks): Your hemodynamics are still stabilizing. Don't introduce new self-management practices until your cardiologist clears you.
  • Active decompensation: If CardiacLens's Decompensation Warning is showing red or multiple signals, a breathing session is not the appropriate response. Call your cardiologist or go to the ER.
  • Severe arrhythmia (uncontrolled): Vagal activation can affect arrhythmia in unpredictable ways depending on the type. If you have an active arrhythmia that your doctor is still managing, ask first.
  • Moderate to severe COPD or lung disease: The breath-hold in the 4-7-8 technique can cause distress. The 4-6 technique without a hold is safer but still discuss it with your pulmonologist.
  • Any symptom during a session: If you experience chest discomfort, dizziness, shortness of breath beyond normal breathing effort, or palpitations during a session — stop immediately. Do not log it as a completed session. Contact your cardiologist.
If you have a pacemaker — read this carefully

The rate floor: Your pacemaker is programmed with a lower rate limit — a minimum HR it will not let your heart drop below (commonly 60 bpm). If your resting HR is at or near this floor, the vagal brake has nothing to work against. The pacemaker will maintain its minimum rate regardless of the parasympathetic signal from breathing. A response of 0–3 bpm in this situation is expected and normal — it does not mean the technique is ineffective for you overall.

Rate-responsive pacing: Some pacemakers adjust their rate based on detected activity or minute ventilation (breathing effort). Deep, slow breathing could theoretically interact with a minute-ventilation sensing pacemaker. This is uncommon but worth asking your device clinic about.

What to ask at your next device check: "What is my lower rate limit set to? Does my device use minute ventilation sensing? Is slow paced breathing appropriate given my current programming?"

A reminder that you have a pacemaker is shown on screen during every active session.

How to run a session — step by step
  1. Sit quietly for 2–3 minutes first. Your pre-session HR should be a true resting value, not a post-activity reading. Walking from the kitchen and immediately logging 72 bpm will inflate your apparent response.
  2. Tap 🫁 Breathing Logger in the Logging section on the main screen.
  3. Take your heart rate and enter it in the Heart Rate Before field.
  4. Select a technique. If this is your first session, choose 4-6 Extended Exhale.
  5. Select a duration. 5 minutes is the standard starting point.
  6. Tap ▶ Start Session. The animated circle guides you — expand your breath as the circle grows, release as it contracts. The INHALE / HOLD / EXHALE text confirms which phase you're in. The cycle counter tracks your progress.
  7. Breathe through your nose if comfortable. Keep your shoulders relaxed. Don't force the exhale — let it fall naturally.
  8. When the countdown reaches zero the session ends automatically. Or tap ■ End to stop early (the actual duration is logged, not the selected duration).
  9. Take your heart rate immediately — within 1–2 minutes of finishing while the vagal response is still active. Enter it in the Heart Rate After field. Your delta appears automatically.
  10. Add a note if anything was unusual about the session, then tap 💾 Save Session.
Session History — what the data tells you

Tap 📋 View Session History to see all past sessions and your personal response profile.

  • Average HR response: Your personal baseline. A response of 8–15 bpm is typical in cardiac patients with elevated sympathetic tone. A response of 0–5 bpm may reflect a pacemaker floor, a low baseline HR, or high parasympathetic tone already.
  • Trend direction: Requires at least 4 sessions. If your last 7 sessions average higher than your overall average, the trend shows as strengthening — consistent practice appears to be building vagal tone. If weaker, it shows declining.
  • Declining response flag: If your recent average drops more than 30% below your overall average, a yellow warning appears. This can indicate increased sympathetic load (illness, stress, poor sleep), a medication change, or a device programming change. Worth noting for your cardiologist.
  • ⚠ Low response marker: Any individual session under 5 bpm is flagged. This does not mean something is wrong — it may just reflect timing, HR measurement variability, or a session near your pacemaker floor.

The history view is cumulative — it shows all sessions ever logged, not just the current date range.

Doctor's Report integration

When you generate a Doctor's Report and breathing sessions exist in the selected date range, a dedicated Breathing Sessions — Vagal Response Log section appears automatically. It includes:

  • Total sessions in the period
  • Average HR response (bpm)
  • Number of sessions that produced a HR decrease
  • Primary technique used
  • Individual session log: date, time, technique, duration, before → after HR, and delta

The report explicitly states that all HR values are patient self-reported from manual pulse check or personal device — no clinical device integration. This protects both you and your cardiologist from over-interpreting the data.

⚠️ Important — the full disclaimer

This tool is for relaxation and pattern awareness only. It is not a medical treatment and does not replace your care team's guidance. HR values are self-reported — math is directional, not definitive.

Do not use this tool if you are experiencing: chest discomfort, shortness of breath beyond normal breathing effort, dizziness, pre-syncope (feeling faint), or a new arrhythmia. These require medical contact, not a breathing session.

Discuss use of breathing techniques with your cardiologist before starting, particularly if you have a pacemaker, ICD, severe arrhythmia, active decompensation, or have been hospitalized in the last 4 weeks.

🌡️ Heat & Humidity Advisory

Why Heat Matters for Cardiac Patients

Your heart already works harder than most. In hot weather, it must also pump extra blood to your skin to cool your body — on top of whatever activity you're doing. This dual demand can trigger dangerous drops in blood pressure, arrhythmias, or acute decompensation, especially with conditions like heart failure, hypertension, or a pacemaker/ICD.

How It Works
1. Log an activity — an optional Outdoor Temperature (°F) field appears in the activity log modal.
2. CardiacLens checks — if the temperature meets or exceeds your advisory threshold and you have heat-sensitive conditions in your profile, an advisory appears after saving.
3. Manual check — use the Run Manual Heat Check button in Settings → Heat & Humidity Advisory to preview an advisory for any temperature without logging an activity.
Heat-Sensitive Conditions Detected

CardiacLens automatically matches conditions in your profile against a list of cardiac and metabolic conditions known to increase heat risk: Heart Failure, Hypertension, Atrial Fibrillation, Coronary Artery Disease, Pacemaker/ICD, Cardiomyopathy, PAD, Diabetes, CKD, COPD, Obesity. Any custom conditions you've entered are also included.

Protective Strategies
  • Pre-hydrate: drink 8–16 oz of water before going outdoors
  • Avoid peak heat hours: 10 AM – 4 PM
  • Cool down for at least 10 minutes after exercise before re-entering an air-conditioned space
  • Watch for warning signs: dizziness, excessive sweating, flushing, rapid or irregular heartbeat, chest discomfort
  • Inform your cardiologist of any new symptoms that appeared during or after heat exposure
Disclaimer: Heat advisories are educational reminders, not medical diagnoses. Your cardiologist sets the final guidance on exercise and heat exposure for your specific situation.

🌅 Morning Check-In & Pattern Tracking

The Problem This Solves

When your doctor says "50 oz of fluid per day," they're giving you a safe baseline number — typically set for your average conditions. They're usually not accounting for your activity level that day, how many hours you'll be awake, whether it's hot outside, or how well you slept the night before. Your body's actual fluid needs vary with all of these things.

CardiacLens can't tell you whether to exceed your limit. But it can track what happens when you do — and over time, build a pattern that you and your doctor can review together to get you a more personalized recommendation.

How It Works

1. Each morning, a 45-second check-in prompt appears on your dashboard.
You tap how you feel (Good / Fair / Rough), note any morning symptoms, and answer a few quick questions about yesterday.

2. The questions focus on yesterday's context — were you physically active? Hot outside? Did you exceed your fluid limit? How did you sleep? Did you eat late?

3. After 7+ days of check-ins, the Doctor's Report gains a new section: Lifestyle & Recovery Patterns. This shows whether you felt better or worse the morning after over-limit fluid days, whether activity is a compensating factor, and how poor sleep affects your next-day symptoms.

4. You bring this report to your cardiologist. Instead of asking "can I drink more on busy days?", you show them 60 days of evidence — and they can give you an informed, personalized answer.

What Gets Tracked
Morning feeling
😊 Good · 😐 Fair · 😟 Rough
Morning symptoms
Swelling, fatigue, breath, fog, heaviness
Yesterday's activity
Active or not, heat exposure
Fluid vs. limit
Over / Within / Under your daily limit
Sleep quality
Good or poor — correlates with BP
Waking hours
Context for fluid needs that day
Where You See the Results
Doctor's Report → Lifestyle & Recovery Patterns section
After 7+ check-ins in the selected period, the Doctor's Report automatically includes pattern analysis — fluid over-limit correlation, sleep impact, top recurring symptoms, and a clinical note for your doctor. Under 7 check-ins, the section shows a placeholder explaining the feature to your doctor.
Advanced Analytics → Morning Patterns panel
Updated in v9.6.5. After 5+ check-ins, the Morning Patterns panel shows feeling distribution, fluid over-limit correlation, sleep quality impact, and top recurring symptoms. New in v9.6.5: symptoms are now classified as New/Worse or Baseline — only new symptoms count in correlation math. Four new Phase 2 charts show fluid intake bands vs. feeling by activity level, new symptom rates on active vs. sedentary days, sleep quality vs. next-morning BP, and late meal days vs. rough morning rate.
⚕️ Important — This is not medical advice

CardiacLens records your self-reported data and identifies patterns. It does not recommend specific fluid amounts, tell you whether to exceed your prescribed limit, or replace your care team's guidance. Use the patterns you collect to have a better-informed conversation with your cardiologist — not to make independent medical decisions.

Enabling / Disabling

Morning Check-In is on by default. To turn it off: Settings → Features → uncheck "🌅 Morning Check-In & Pattern Tracking." When off, no prompt appears and no data is collected. If you turn it back on, previously collected check-ins are still available and will appear in future reports.

🔒 What You See When CardiacLens Resets Each Morning

When you open CardiacLens after midnight — whether first thing in the morning or after leaving it running overnight — a blue banner appears at the top of the screen and walks through a short sequence as the app prepares your new day.

One of those steps shows you your real record count: total BP readings, days of history, symptoms logged, medications on file, and appointments saved. This is a live scan — the number you see is the actual count stored on this device right now. When those numbers appear, you know your data is intact, untouched, and exactly where you left it.

You can run the same check manually at any time from the bottom of Settings → 🔒 Privacy & Data Check.

FAQ

🔒 What is Secure Access?

Secure Access is an optional privacy feature that locks the entire CardiacLens app behind a personal key letter. When enabled, CardiacLens opens to a PIN entry screen every time it is launched. Anyone who picks up your phone and opens the app will see what looks like a numeric PIN pad — they will tap numbers and never get in. You unlock it by typing your chosen key letter instead of a number. Set it up in Settings → 🔒 Secure Access.

🔑 What if I forget my key letter?

On the lock screen, tap "Forgot your code?" three times. Your security question will appear. Answer it correctly and your key letter will be shown to you. Write it down somewhere safe before continuing. If you also forget your security question answer, you will need to delete and reinstall the app — make sure you have a current backup before that happens.

📱 Can someone bypass Secure Access if they have my phone?

Secure Access protects against casual snooping — a stranger who finds your phone, a wrong recipient of a forwarded message, or someone who briefly picks up your device. It is not a replacement for your phone's built-in passcode or biometric lock, and a technically skilled person with extended physical access to the device could potentially bypass it. For the vast majority of real-world privacy threats, Secure Access provides meaningful protection. Use your phone's own passcode as the first line of defense.

📱 Do I need to do anything when a new version is released?

Yes — always export your data before updating. Go to Device Sync & Backup → Export ALL Data and save the file. Then update, then import your data back. This takes about 60 seconds and protects everything you've recorded. The app will show you an update banner and walk you through the steps.

💾 How often should I back up my data?

At minimum, before any app update and once a week as a habit. Also back up before clearing your browser cache or before deleting the CardiacLens icon from your Home Screen — both of those actions erase your local data. Go to Device Sync & Backup → Export ALL Data.

📥 I imported my data but nothing appeared — what do I do?

After importing, the app reloads automatically. If your data still doesn't appear: close the app completely and reopen it. On any phone or tablet, close the app fully and reopen it. On any desktop or laptop browser, close the tab and open a fresh one at cardiaclens.com. On iPhone/iPad specifically, swipe up from the bottom and swipe away CardiacLens before reopening. Your .json backup file is safe — you can import it again as many times as needed on any device.

🔄 Will my data transfer automatically when a new version releases?

Not automatically — you need to export before updating and import after. This is intentional: CardiacLens never moves your data without you taking a deliberate action. Your health data stays under your control at all times.

📊 What are the four Advanced Analytics detectors?

CardiacLens has four pattern detectors that analyze your historical data — not just today's readings. The HR Rate Detector finds sustained heart rate shifts and sudden changes. The Decompensation Warning monitors five clinical signs of CHF fluid overload building simultaneously. The Pulse Pressure Detector tracks the gap between your systolic and diastolic readings as a cardiac output indicator. The Symptom Convergence Detector finds clusters, escalations, and clinical patterns across your symptom history. All four are in the Analytics section of the main page.

📌 What is a Pinned Event and why should I use it?

When a detector finds a significant pattern, it suggests pinning it. A pinned event is permanently saved — it never disappears when your 90-day data window rolls forward. It captures the detector's finding, the event date, and all the raw data (BP readings, symptoms, weight, notes) from that day. Pinned events appear in your Doctor's Report as a dedicated section, in the 60-day Timeline as amber markers, and in the Pinned Events viewer under Analytics. Think of pins as your pre-prepared clinical evidence for your next cardiologist appointment.

🗂️ What should I do with pinned events after my cardiologist appointment?

After your appointment, open the Pinned Events viewer and tap "Archive & Clear." This permanently saves all current pins to an archive (you can always view them), then clears your active list so the next period starts fresh. CardiacLens will remind you automatically — if you have an upcoming appointment within 14 days, or if you just had one this week, a banner appears on the main page and inside the Pinned Events modal. If no appointment is logged but your oldest pin is 60+ days old, you'll see a gentle nudge to archive and refresh.

🔢 What does "math is directional, not definitive" mean?

It means CardiacLens's detectors can tell you that a pattern exists in your data — but they cannot tell you why it exists or what it means for your specific medical situation. A detected HR level shift is real math — but it could reflect a clinical change, a medication adjustment taking effect, seasonal variation, or something entirely benign. Your cardiologist provides the interpretation. CardiacLens provides the evidence and the pattern recognition to make that conversation richer and more productive than walking in with just a blood pressure number.

💾 How should I think about long-term data preservation?

CardiacLens keeps 90 days of rolling data on your device. For long-term preservation, export your data as a backup at the end of each quarter — the last day of March, June, September, and December. Save these files somewhere safe (iCloud, Google Drive, email to yourself). A new cardiologist years from now could import your Q3 2026 backup and see your complete cardiac picture from that period. Pinned events in each backup capture the most significant findings for that quarter, pre-annotated for clinical review.

📶 Do I need Wi-Fi or cellular for CardiacLens to work?

No. Almost everything works offline — BP logging, medication logging, reminders, Sentinel, all detectors, Doctor’s Report, Deep Dive, Pinned Events, and Meal Library. Only Ask CardiacLens requires internet. You can run CardiacLens in Airplane Mode.

One thing to know about reminders in Airplane Mode: the screen must stay on and lit, and Low Power Mode must be off. Airplane Mode does not affect timers — but a black screen or Low Power Mode will. If an event is missed because the screen went off, the app will show you a recovery screen when you reopen it.

Why this is on iOS specifically: This is a platform restriction, not a CardiacLens limitation. On Android, Chrome allows an installed web app's service worker to keep running and receive push notifications even after the app is fully closed. On iOS, Safari suspends all JavaScript for installed web apps the instant they leave the foreground — there is no equivalent capability. iOS 16.4+ added Web Push, but it can only deliver a notification through Apple's push service; it can't run CardiacLens's actual reminder logic in the background. Replicating Android's reliability on iOS would require CardiacLens to run a server and add an account system — which would mean your data leaving your device. We chose to keep CardiacLens serverless and account-free instead.

🔔 Will reminders fire with the screen off or in Low Power Mode?

No to both — on iOS. Screen off suspends the timer. Low Power Mode throttles timers even when the app is visible. Keep screen on, Low Power Mode off. Airplane Mode is fine.

This isn't something CardiacLens can fix — iOS Safari does not allow installed web apps to execute code in the background, a capability Android's Chrome does extend to installed web apps. If you do miss events because the screen was off, CardiacLens recovers automatically — when you reopen the app, a Missed While Screen Was Off screen will appear listing every event that was skipped. You can run each one in order or skip it. Nothing is silently lost.

🚨 A detector showed a red warning — should I go to the emergency room?

No — CardiacLens findings are not emergency indicators. A red finding means the pattern in your data is statistically significant and worth discussing with your cardiologist promptly — not that you are in immediate danger. If you are experiencing chest pain, severe shortness of breath, sudden dizziness, or other acute symptoms, call 911 or go to an emergency room regardless of what CardiacLens shows. CardiacLens is for pattern tracking over time, not acute symptom assessment.

💬 How do I send feedback or report a problem?

Tap the purple Send Feedback button on the main screen. Standard users can report an issue (routed to support@cardiaclens.com) or volunteer to become a beta tester. Beta testers receive a weekly check-in prompt and send feedback directly to the developer at betatesting@cardiaclens.com.

🧪 What is the beta tester program?

Beta testers commit to a weekly check-in — a brief honest response about their experience, even if it's “all good.” Two consecutive ignored prompts automatically graduate you from the program with no option to re-enroll. This keeps the feedback channel meaningful. To join, tap Send Feedback → Become a Beta Tester.

Privacy & About CardiacLens

Your Data Never Leaves Your Device

CardiacLens stores everything — your blood pressure readings, symptoms, medications, weight, fluid logs, notes, and pinned events — exclusively in your browser's local storage on the device you are using right now. There is no server. There is no account. There is no cloud sync. Nothing you enter is ever transmitted anywhere.

✅ What CardiacLens Collects
  • Health data you enter manually: BP readings, symptoms, weight, fluid intake, medications, activities, meals, notes
  • Settings and preferences you configure: conditions, BP targets, fluid limits, event schedules, medication list
  • Pinned events and their communication logs — stored locally and included in your exported backup file
  • Anonymous usage analytics (Google Analytics 4) — page views, feature usage, general app performance, and technical error diagnostics (the error message, which file and line number, and a short code stack trace) if something goes wrong, so issues can be found and fixed faster. No personally identifiable information, no health data values — error diagnostics describe what the code was doing, never your BP readings, medications, notes, or any other entered data.
🚫 What CardiacLens Never Does
  • Never sends your health data to any server, database, or third party
  • Never creates an account, requires login, or tracks your identity
  • Never sells, shares, or monetizes your information in any form
  • Never displays advertisements
  • Never requires an internet connection to function — works fully offline after first load
✈️ Airplane Mode — The Ultimate Privacy Posture

CardiacLens stores everything on-device. Run it entirely in Airplane Mode — every feature works with all radios off. Only Ask CardiacLens requires internet.

Screen on, Airplane Mode on, Low Power Mode off: your data stays on device, no signal leaves.

If the screen does go off and you miss a scheduled event, CardiacLens will surface a Missed While Screen Was Off recovery screen the moment you reopen the app — nothing is silently lost.

🔒 Secure Access — App Lock

Secure Access adds a lock screen to CardiacLens. When enabled, the app opens to what looks like a numeric PIN pad every time it is launched. The actual unlock mechanism is a letter you choose — not a number. Anyone who doesn't know this will tap numbers and never get past the lock screen.

What it protects against: A stranger who finds your phone, casual snooping by someone who borrows your device, or a forwarded export file being read by the wrong person.

How to set it up: Go to Settings → 🔒 Secure Access. Choose your key letter, choose a security question, and confirm. The lock screen activates immediately.

If you forget your key letter: Tap "Forgot your code?" three times on the lock screen. Your security question appears. Answer it correctly and your key letter is shown to you.

🔒 Who Can See Your Data

Only you — on the device where CardiacLens is installed. If you export your data and share the .json file with someone (a family member, caregiver, or doctor), that person can import and view it. That sharing is entirely under your control and only happens when you initiate it.

If you add CardiacLens to your Home Screen on iPhone or iPad, be aware that the local storage is tied to that browser and device. Removing the icon erases the stored data. Always export a backup before removing the icon or clearing browser data.

🏥 Why We Built It This Way

CardiacLens was built by a cardiac patient after a hospitalization, motivated by a simple question: why isn't there a tool that tracks all the things my cardiologist actually needs to see — BP trends, symptoms, fluid balance, medication adherence, and pattern detection — in one place, for free, without giving up my health data?

The answer was to build it. The privacy model — all data on-device, no accounts, no servers — is not a limitation. It is a deliberate choice rooted in the belief that your cardiac health data is yours, belongs to no one else, and should never become a product.

🔒 Privacy & Data Check — Verify Any Time

CardiacLens includes a built-in Privacy & Data Check you can run whenever you want. Find it at the bottom of Settings — tap 🔒 Run Privacy & Data Check.

When it runs, CardiacLens scans your stored data and shows you the actual count: how many BP readings, how many days of history, how many symptoms logged, medications on file, and appointments saved. You see your real numbers — not a generic reassurance. If those numbers match what you know you've entered, your data is intact and untouched.

The check also confirms what CardiacLens is built to do: your data lives only on this device and is never sent anywhere. That is an architectural fact — there is no server for it to go to.

The same verification runs automatically every morning when CardiacLens resets for your new day, so you see your record count each time you open the app after midnight.

⚕️ Medical Disclaimer

CardiacLens is a personal health tracking tool, not a medical device. It is not FDA-cleared, CE-marked, or certified as a clinical diagnostic system. It does not provide medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment recommendations.

Accuracy statement: All pattern detection is statistical and based on self-reported data. Findings are directional, not definitive — they identify patterns worth discussing with your cardiologist, not conclusions about your medical condition. Results may be affected by data entry errors, device measurement variability, and individual clinical factors the app cannot account for.

Always consult your cardiologist or other qualified healthcare provider before making any changes to your care plan based on information from CardiacLens.

CardiacLens v9.10.274 · Free & Source-Available · Built by a cardiac patient, for cardiac patients · cardiaclens.com
No ads · No account · No subscription · Your data stays on your device · Always free

🔒 Secure Access

What is Secure Access?

Secure Access locks the entire CardiacLens app behind a private visual keyboard. When enabled, CardiacLens opens to a colorful 24-key grid every time it launches.

Anyone who picks up your phone sees the keyboard — but has no idea what to do with it. Only you know how to get in.

📱 What you see when the app opens

A 24-key grid fills the screen. Every key has a unique color and a unique symbol. The layout reshuffles every time the app opens.

CARDIACLENS — SECURE ACCESS
Q
7
M
B
3
X
9
F
2
G
K
T
4
W
R
8
P
5
A
1
N
H
6
C
Forgot your combination?

Every key has a color and a symbol. The grid reshuffles every time the app opens.

The 8 colors — each with its own symbol
Red — Circle
Blue — Square
Green — Triangle
Orange — Diamond
Purple — Star
Teal — Hexagon
Pink — Heart
Gold — Plus
🔐 Setting up Secure Access
  1. Go to Settings → 🔒 Secure Access
  2. Choose your letter — any letter A–Z
  3. Choose your color — pick whichever you'll remember
  4. Set a security question for recovery
  5. Tap Activate Secure Access
Write your letter and color down and keep them somewhere safe — not on this device.
🔒 Locking the app

Tap the 🔒 100% Private badge in the header. The keyboard appears immediately. Walk away.

❓ Forgot your combination?

Tap "Forgot your combination?" at the bottom of the keyboard screen. Answer your security question and your combination will be shown. You will be required to set a new one before the app opens.

If you also forget your security question answer, contact CardiacLens support. Do not attempt to reinstall — your health data will be preserved if you reach out first.
✅ What Secure Access protects against
  • A stranger who finds your lost or stolen phone
  • Anyone who picks up your device and opens CardiacLens
  • Casual snooping by family members, visitors, or coworkers
⚠️ What it does not protect against
  • Someone who has watched you unlock the app
  • A technically skilled person with extended device access

Use Secure Access alongside your phone's own passcode or biometrics — not instead of them.

💡 Sharing access with a trusted person

Tell them your letter and color verbally — in person or by phone. Never by text or email. Knowing both is the authorization.

🗂️ Data Management

Use the tools below to clear your health data stored on this device. These actions cannot be undone.

🔒 Want to protect your data before sharing or stepping away?

Use Secure Access — tap the 🔓 button at the bottom-left of the app to lock it instantly. Set it up in Settings → 🔒 Secure Access or see the Security tab in Help for full details.

🔄 Reset Today's Data

Removes only today's BP readings, symptoms, meals, activity, dismissed events, and daily plan. Your settings, medications, procedures, appointments, and all previous days are kept.

⚠️ Delete ALL Data on This Device

Permanently erases everything: all daily readings (every day), settings, medications, procedures, appointments, custom symptoms/activities, analytics notes, and reminders. The app will return to a fresh-install state.

💡 Tip: Use "Reset Today" if you entered test data and want a clean start for the day. Use "Delete All" only if you want to completely start over or before giving someone else this device.

💾 When to Back Up Your Data

Your data lives only on this device. Back up regularly so you never lose it.

  • Before every app update — always export your data before installing a new version
  • Before clearing browser cache — clearing Safari or Chrome data wipes CardiacLens data too
  • Before deleting the Home Screen icon — removing the icon erases all data on iOS
  • Weekly — as a general habit, export once a week for peace of mind

How to back up: Go to Device Sync & Backup → Export ALL Data. Save the .json file to your Files app, iCloud, or email it to yourself.

🔄 How to Update to a New Version

When a new version is available, the app will show a banner at the top. Follow these steps in order — skipping step 1 risks losing your data.

  1. Export your data first — Device Sync & Backup → Export ALL Data → save the file
  2. Tap the update banner and follow the instructions to open the new version
  3. Once the new version loads, go to Device Sync & Backup → Import All Data
  4. Select your saved .json file — your data will be restored
  5. Verify your readings and settings appear correctly before using the app

📥 If Your Imported Data Doesn't Appear

After importing, the app reloads automatically. If your data still doesn't appear:

  • Wait for the page to fully finish reloading before checking
  • If data is still missing, close the app completely and reopen it
  • On iPhone: swipe up from the bottom, swipe away CardiacLens, then reopen
  • On Chromebook: close the tab and open a fresh one at cardiaclens.com
  • If data still doesn't appear, try the import again — your .json file is safe to re-use

Known Issues

This tab tracks bugs that were reported and fixed — things that were broken and are now resolved. Each entry describes the defective behavior, the root cause, and the fix applied.

For new features and capabilities, see the Planned Features tab.

📱 Platform note: iOS reminder limitations aren't a CardiacLens bug

Several reports over the life of this project have come down to the same root cause: iOS Safari suspends all background JavaScript for installed web apps, so reminders stop firing when the screen is off or Low Power Mode is on. Android's Chrome extends background service workers and push notifications to installed web apps; Apple does not extend the equivalent capability to web apps — only to apps distributed through the App Store, on which Apple takes a commission. CardiacLens could match Android's reliability with a server-based push architecture, but that would require an account system and a server, breaking the on-device-only, no-account privacy model. We've chosen privacy over reminder reliability on iOS. See the FAQ for the full explanation and the workaround (screen on, Low Power Mode off).

🧪 New in v9.10.274 — June 17, 2026
  • Service worker — updateViaCache fix. v9.10.268 attempted to force SW updates using a versioned query string (sw.js?v=9.10.268). iOS Safari rejected this with a hard error: "Cannot update a service worker with a requested script URL whose newest worker has a different script URL." Reverted to ./sw.js and instead added updateViaCache: 'none' to the registration options. This is the correct iOS-compatible approach — it instructs the browser to always bypass HTTP cache when fetching sw.js to check for updates, regardless of the cache headers GitHub Pages sends. The script URL stays the same so iOS accepts it, but the update check always hits the network.
🧪 New in v9.10.268 — June 17, 2026
  • Service worker — version-pinned registration URL. GitHub Pages CDN was caching the old sw.js with long cache headers, so the browser never re-fetched it and never discovered new versions. Users continued running stale cached HTML indefinitely. Fixed: the service worker is now registered as ./sw.js?v=9.10.268. The browser treats a changed query string as a new URL and always bypasses HTTP cache to fetch it. Every future version bump changes the query string, guaranteeing the new service worker is fetched and installed immediately on next app open. No user action ever required.
🧪 New in v9.10.267 — June 17, 2026
  • Service worker — automatic update fix. The previous service worker served cached HTML even after a new version was deployed to GitHub Pages, so users continued running the old version until they manually cleared Safari's website data. Fixed: the service worker now fetches HTML files (index.html and the root path) with cache: 'no-store', bypassing the CDN and browser cache entirely.
